title: Psalms 134
subtitle: Praise the LORD, All You Servants of the LORD

sections.header: Verses 1–2 — “You Who Minister by Night in the House of the LORD”

heb.paragraph: "Praise the LORD, all you servants of the LORD who minister by night (lēlōṭ) in the house of the LORD" (v.1). The final Song of Ascent. A nighttime psalm: the Levites who served the night watch in the temple are summoned to praise. "Lift up your hands in the sanctuary and praise the LORD" (v.2). Hands raised in the dark temple — worship when no one else sees.

notes.note: "All you servants who minister by NIGHT. Lift up your hands and praise." Night worship: the most hidden, most faithful form of praise. No audience, no daylight, no crowd — just the night-watch servants and God. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

refs.note: Day and night the living creatures never stop saying, 'Holy, holy, holy.'
Levitical singers were on duty day and night, exempt from other tasks.
I want people everywhere to pray, lifting up holy hands.

hist.context: Psalm 134 concludes the Songs of Ascents as a night liturgy. Pilgrims call upon temple servants to bless the LORD during night watches. 'You who serve by night in the house of the LORD' suggests Levitical choirs maintaining worship around the clock. The brief psalm (3 verses) functions as a closing benediction for the entire collection.

sections.header: Verse 3 — “May the LORD Bless You from Zion”

heb.paragraph: "May the LORD bless you (yəbārek̅ək̅ā) from Zion, he who is the Maker of heaven and earth" (v.3). The night-watch Levites are blessed by the pilgrim. The pilgrimage that began in exile (Ps 120) ends in the temple at night (Ps 134). The final word of the Songs of Ascent: the Creator of heaven and earth blesses from Zion. Three verses close fifteen psalms of ascending.

notes.note: "May the LORD bless you from Zion, he who is the Maker of heaven and earth." The pilgrim blesses the night-servants. The Maker of heaven and earth blesses from the small hill of Zion. Cosmic Creator, local dwelling. The Songs of Ascent end. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

refs.note: The priestly blessing: 'The LORD bless you and keep you.'
My help comes from the LORD, the Maker of heaven and earth.
God sent his servant to bless you—the blessing extending from Zion to all nations.

hist.context: The psalm ends with a priestly response: 'May the LORD bless you from Zion, he who is the Maker of heaven and earth.' The pilgrimage concludes with blessing flowing outward from temple to pilgrim, from Zion to the world. The Ascent collection's final word is blessing—the goal of every pilgrimage, the gift Jerusalem bestows on departing worshipers.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: No audience, no daylight, no crowd — just the night-watch servants and God…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVMay the Lord bless you from Zion, he who is the Maker of heaven and earth.ESVMay the LORD bless you from Zion, he who made heaven and earth!NIVPraise the Lord, all you servants of the Lord who minister by night in the house of the Lord.ESVCome, bless the LORD, all you servants of the LORD, who stand by night in the house of the LORD!

rec.text: ""All you servants who minister by NIGHT. Lift up your hands and praise." Night worship: the most hidden, most faithful form of praise. No audience, no daylight, no crowd — just the night-watch servant…"

rows.label: Blessed from Zion — 134:3
rows.text: "May the LORD BLESS you from Zion." The Maker of heaven and earth. The Songs of Ascent END.

hebtext.note: "Praise the LORD, all you servants of the LORD who minister by night (lēlōṭ) in the house of the LORD" (v.1). The final Song of Ascent. A nighttime psalm: the Levites who served the night watch in the temple are summoned to praise. "Lift up your hands in the sanctuary and praise the LORD" (v.2). Hands raised in the dark temple — worship when no one else sees.

"May the LORD bless you (yəbārek̅ək̅ā) from Zion, he who is the Maker of heaven and earth" (v.3). The night-watch Levites are blessed by the pilgrim. The pilgrimage that began in exile (Ps 120) ends in the temple at night (Ps 134). The final word of the Songs of Ascent: the Creator of heaven and earth blesses from Zion. Three verses close fifteen psalms of ascending.

themes.note: Worship peaks: night-watch praise. The Songs of Ascent close at the temple in the dark. The pilgrimage from exile (Ps 120) to the night-watch (Ps 134) is complete.

prayer_prompt: Lift up your hands in the sanctuary and praise the LORD. Consider asking God about your nighttime worship — do you praise Him when others are asleep and no one sees?
